title: INNER JOIN — combine employees with their departments
instruction:
The `employees` table stores `department_id` — a number. But you want to see the department name. `JOIN` connects the two tables through that shared key.

This is the most fundamental JOIN query you'll write in any system.
starter:
SELECT
  e.name        AS employee,
  e.job_title,
  e.salary,
  d.name        AS department,
  d.location
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
ORDER BY d.name, e.salary DESC;
expectedOutput: Eva Martinez|Head of Data Science|195000|Data Science|San Francisco
successMessage: 15 rows — every employee matched with their department. The `ON e.department_id = d.id` is the join condition. `e` and `d` are table aliases that make the query shorter and clearer.

title: Multiple JOINs — orders, customers, and revenue
instruction:
Real business queries join 3 or more tables. The pattern is the same: each JOIN adds a new table connected through a shared key.

Build a revenue report that shows customer name, country, product, and total spend.
starter:
SELECT
  c.name          AS customer,
  c.country,
  o.product,
  COUNT(o.id)     AS order_count,
  SUM(o.amount)   AS total_spent
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id
WHERE o.status = 'paid'
GROUP BY c.name, c.country, o.product
ORDER BY total_spent DESC
LIMIT 8;
successMessage: Momentum Co is the top customer with 3 Enterprise Plan renewals ($72k). This query joins `orders` → `customers`, filters to paid orders, then aggregates by customer and product. Real CRM reports look exactly like this.

title: LEFT JOIN — find what's missing
instruction:
`LEFT JOIN` returns ALL rows from the left table, plus matching rows from the right. When there's no match, the right-side columns are `NULL`.

Find all customers and their total spending — including customers who haven't ordered yet.
starter:
SELECT
  c.name                         AS customer,
  c.country,
  COUNT(o.id)                    AS order_count,
  COALESCE(SUM(o.amount), 0)     AS total_spent
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id AND o.status = 'paid'
GROUP BY c.name, c.country
ORDER BY total_spent DESC;
successMessage: NovaSoft and Vertex Solutions appear with 0 orders — they signed up but never completed a paid purchase (their orders are `pending`). `LEFT JOIN` surfaces this. An `INNER JOIN` would have silently excluded them.
